scenario_results.reason: Normal in-scope ambulatory reporting produced explicit metric definitions, attribution logic, source precedence, caveats, and action thresholds grounded in schedule, encounter, claims, registry, and survey records.
Ambiguous no-show logic was blocked until the tenant definition and source hierarchy were clarified.
Missing claims, stale scheduling data, and incomplete survey files triggered a low-confidence escalation instead of a smoothed clinic trend claim.
Scheduling, EHR, and paid-claim disagreements were preserved as a reconciliation case with no single authoritative metric asserted.
The lane refused to turn descriptive access and productivity trends into a causal staffing or policy claim and named the evidence gaps required for stronger inference.
Unsupported benchmark and disclosure language was refused and routed to quality, compliance, privacy, and disclosure owners with unresolved records named explicitly.
Missing specialty scope, attribution model, system-of-record mapping, and retrieval hooks produced a blocked-prerequisites response instead of implied comparability.
A generic wellness-app and medspa funnel request was rejected cleanly so the ambulatory label did not become cosmetic.
Certification, compensation, coding-policy, and clinical-judgment authority were rejected cleanly and routed to the correct adjacent specialists.
Ambulatory terminology was normalized to explicit attribution, POS, E/M, registry, and survey artifacts without collapsing into generic analytics language.
Tenant-specific metric adaptation was recorded explicitly while preserving the public baseline definition and comparison posture.
Missing disclosure approval, minimum-necessary review, and peer-review release authority prevented unsupported clinician-level publication and forced escalation.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to ambulatory clinic metrics, attribution and visit-state workflow, public-reporting posture, and adjacent-authority escalation.
Functionality coverage is complete for all shared abilities, all analytics-reporting-and-disclosure abilities, and the cross-industry ability required by the functionality contract.
